Maladaptive Strategies.value: I detach myself from the memories
Situations in which other people evaluate me / my appearance / my performance

Threat.value: I listen for sounds that might mean someone is entering my home
I notice potential weapons or dangerous objects in any environment
I jump or startle at ordinary sounds that would not bother most people

vignette:
Arthur is a 73-year-old divorced auditor of Oceania ethnicity who presents with persistent post-traumatic stress symptoms, with a PCL-5 score of 64. He describes a traumatic incident in which armed men in military-style and police-style uniforms confronted him, with weapons visible and the atmosphere of the encounter feeling immediately dangerous. At the peak of danger, he stood still and tried not to attract attention, unsure how to defend himself or get away. Uniforms and weapons now stand out to him in a way that feels unmistakable, and in his auditing work he notices them in client buildings, foyers, and security areas.

When Arthur talks about the event, he does not give a smooth account. He repeatedly stops on one image, saying he can “see that part again,” and his voice changes as though he is looking at the scene rather than describing it. He then goes quiet or detaches himself from what he has just said, speaking in a flat, distant way before returning to the same fragment. At other times he puts the story aside entirely and speaks about it as if it happened to someone else.

Arthur’s central stuck point is, in his own words, “I was helpless, and I’m still not safe.” He speaks as though the trauma proved that danger can arrive without warning and that he cannot rely on himself to manage it. He also expresses a belief that other people, especially figures linked to authority, may not be protective or trustworthy. When he thinks about the event, he sounds as if the world remains a place where he must assume threat.

He listens for sounds that might mean someone is entering his home, particularly at night, and he jumps at ordinary noises that do not bother most people. In unfamiliar settings he notices potential weapons or dangerous objects almost automatically. He avoids military and police uniforms, and he avoids situations in which other people evaluate his appearance or performance. These pressures show up in his work as an auditor, where he becomes tense during formal reviews and client-facing checks.

Arthur’s experience is held in a self-reinforcing pattern. The sharp image of the confrontation returns, and that vivid memory leaves him more convinced that he is vulnerable and that the world is unsafe. Those beliefs keep him scanning for danger, listening at the door, and watching for weapons or uniforms. The heightened sense of threat then makes the memory feel closer and more intrusive again, and the same appraisals about helplessness and danger become even harder for him to shake. In his auditing role, this leaves him on edge while entering unfamiliar offices and sitting through performance scrutiny, with little room to settle.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
Arthur is a 73-year-old divorced auditor of Oceania ethnicity who presents with persistent post-traumatic stress symptoms, with a PCL-5 score of 64. He describes a trauma in which he was threatened by armed men in a setting where uniforms and weapons were impossible to miss; at the peak of danger he felt he had no safe way to challenge them or get away, and he remembers standing rigidly while trying not to draw attention to himself. Since then, military and police uniforms, as well as visible weapons, have become especially potent reminders for him. In his current work as an auditor, he notices these cues immediately in public buildings and at client sites, scanning entrances and security desks before he can settle into the task at hand.

When Arthur speaks about the event, he does not tell it as a continuous story. He breaks off, returns to one sharp moment, and seems pulled back to the sight of the armed figures as if the rest of the scene drops away. He says he can “see that part again” and then struggles to carry the account forward, as though the memory arrives in fragments rather than as a completed episode. He often detaches himself from the memory altogether, describing it in a distant, matter-of-fact way before pausing and going quiet.

Arthur’s central stuck point is that the trauma confirmed a harsh belief that he is not safe and not capable of protecting himself: “If it happened once, it can happen again, and I would be helpless.” He also speaks as if other people and public authority figures cannot be trusted to keep him secure. When he recalls his fear, he becomes more convinced that the world remains dangerous and that he must stay on guard.

In daily life, Arthur listens for sounds that might mean someone is entering his home, especially at night. He jumps at ordinary noises that other people seem to ignore, and in unfamiliar places he immediately notices anything that could be used as a weapon or look threatening. He avoids military and police uniforms and steers away from situations where other people might evaluate his appearance or performance, which has made some client meetings at work more stressful and has left him feeling more exposed.

A self-reinforcing cycle is apparent. The memory of the armed confrontation brings back his sense of danger, and that threat state sharpens the memory again, making it feel closer and more immediate. Once the memory feels vivid, Arthur becomes more convinced that he is vulnerable and that the world is unsafe; those negative appraisals then keep his vigilance high, with constant scanning for sounds, exits, and weapons. The cycle is especially difficult for him in auditing work, where he must enter unfamiliar offices and be assessed on his accuracy and judgment, leaving him tense before he even starts.

violations.explanation: Violated by the sentence: "In his current work as an auditor, he notices these cues immediately in public buildings and at client sites, scanning entrances and security desks before he can settle into the task at hand." This sentence names the trigger-like cues and the maladaptive strategy (scanning) with causal/sequence language implied by 'before he can settle,' but it does not explicitly state Triggers as the source of the strategy in the required way.
